brands.name: 1-800-GOT-JUNK?
brands.notes: Junk-removal pioneer founded 1989 in Vancouver (parent O2E Brands), franchising since 1998 with ~166 locations. Strong unit economics (median ~$3.1M gross revenue per franchised business), but US territories are effectively sold out and combined ongoing fees are high.

riskFlags.title: US territories effectively sold out
riskFlags.detail: The franchisor's own development page states the brand 'sold out' and that availability arises mainly when an existing franchise partner decides to sell; it steers prospects to sister brands WOW 1 DAY PAINTING and Shack Shine. New entry is mostly via resale or adding subterritories to existing owners.

riskFlags.title: High combined ongoing fees vs category norms
riskFlags.detail: Entrepreneur lists an 8% royalty plus 6% ad royalty plus 2% technology fee (~16% combined of gross sales), well above the 8-10% combined burden typical of home-services franchises.

riskFlags.title: Franchisee termination litigation (Millennium Asset Recovery)
riskFlags.detail: In 1-800-Got Junk? LLC v. Superior Court (Cal. Ct. App. 2010), a Southern California franchisee terminated for under-reporting revenue sued for wrongful termination; the court let the franchisee proceed under the Washington-law choice-of-law clause, which limits summary termination without cure. Overall disclosed litigation volume in the FDD is low.
riskFlags.source: Quimbee case brief, 1-800-Got Junk? LLC v. Superior Court, 116 Cal. Rptr. 3d 923

liquidCapitalMin.note: Entrepreneur 'cash requirement'; FranchiseInvestorData lists $40K liquid capital — sources conflict.

franchiseFee.note: Range $65,000-$97,500; minimum purchase is 8 subterritories for the $65,000 minimum initial fee.

adFundPercent.note: Plus a 2% technology fee per Entrepreneur.

avgUnitRevenue.note: Median annual gross revenue per US franchised business per 2025 FDD Item 19 (147 reporting US franchised businesses); many franchise partners run multiple subterritories. Conflict: VettedBiz reports ~$2.03M yearly gross sales; Franchise Chatter's 2024 FDD review reported $3.06M average sales.

totalUnits.note: Entrepreneur count; 2025 FDD shows 147 US franchised businesses (per FranchiseInvestorData) and VettedBiz lists 146 US locations.

trainingWeeks.note: Approximately 34 hours total (8 on-the-job + 26 classroom) per Entrepreneur, i.e. under one week.

item19Available.note: 2025 FDD includes Item 19 financial performance representations.

veteranDiscount.note: Entrepreneur lists veteran incentives as N/A.

multiUnitAvailable.note: Expansion model is built on adding subterritories to existing franchise partners.

canRunFromHome.note: Can be run from home/mobile unit.

isGrowing.note: Marginal unit growth (+0.6% per Entrepreneur); growth comes mostly from subterritory expansion rather than new franchisees.

ownerTypes.note: Absentee ownership not permitted; owner-operator model.

value: Consolidated contact center (sales, dispatch, field support)

brands.name: Budget Blinds
brands.notes: Largest US window-coverings franchise (parent Home Franchise Concepts), founded 1992, franchising since 1994, with roughly 1,500 home-based territories across the US and Canada and strong recent unit growth (+7.1%). Flat-fee royalty structure and low overhead, but single-territory revenue is modest and there is a documented history of franchisee complaints.

riskFlags.title: Franchisee complaints: terminations, churning allegations, fee burden
riskFlags.detail: Unhappy Franchisee documents former franchisees alleging the franchisor terminated owners and resold territories ('churning'), kept a late royalty payment while terminating anyway, and that flat monthly fees ($1,500-$2,000 per territory after first-year waivers) left small operators barely breaking even. These are unverified franchisee allegations, not adjudicated findings.
riskFlags.source: Unhappy Franchisee — Budget Blinds franchise complaints

riskFlags.title: Historical system attrition (2010-2014)
riskFlags.detail: Per an Unhappy Franchisee analysis of FDD data, of 939 Budget Blinds franchises, about 163 (17%) were terminated, not renewed, reacquired, or ceased operation between 2010 and 2014. The system has since returned to strong growth (1,362 to 1,489 US units in 2025 per FranchiseInvestorData).
riskFlags.source: Unhappy Franchisee — Budget Blinds franchise horror story

riskFlags.title: Litigation disclosed in FDD
riskFlags.detail: VettedBiz notes the franchise discloses lawsuits and/or bankruptcy information in its FDD (Items 3-4); prospective buyers should review the current FDD for specifics.

nationwide.source: Budget Blinds franchise site — Available Territories
nationwide.note: Franchisor actively markets territories of 25,000-36,000 households via a US availability map with no published state exclusions; ~1,500 territories already operate across the US and Canada, so some areas are resale-only.

liquidCapitalMin.note: Entrepreneur cash requirement; franchisor financing of up to $56,000 available for qualified candidates.

totalInvestmentMin.note: Range $100,500-$211,250; matches franchisor's own franchise site.

franchiseFee.note: Initial franchise fee $19,950 plus a separate $70,000 initial territory fee (~$89,950 combined upfront).

royaltyPercent.note: Entrepreneur lists 3.5%+ with an escalating flat monthly structure ($300-$2,000/month by tenure); ad fund is a flat $500-$1,500/month rather than a percentage, so adFundPercent is omitted.

avgUnitRevenue.note: Mean gross sales for single-territory US franchisees per FDD Item 19 (2026 FDD, effective April 2026; 2024-2025 data). Median across territories is ~$540K and multi-territory (3+) operators average $2.5M+; VettedBiz reports ~$557K yearly gross sales — distribution is heavily skewed by territory count.

totalUnits.note: FranchiseInvestorData reports 1,489 US units after +127 net growth in 2025; ~1,500 incl. Canada per franchisor.

trainingWeeks.note: Two-week program (93.5 classroom hours) combining hands-on, live, and virtual training.

item19Available.note: Item 19 reports gross sales by territory count cohort.

veteranDiscount.note: 15% off the initial franchise fee for qualified veterans and eligible spouses.

multiUnitAvailable.note: Item 19 breaks out multi-territory (3+) operators, who average $2.5M+ in gross sales.

canRunFromHome.note: Franchisor markets a home-office, low-overhead, no-retail-space model.

isGrowing.note: +7.1% unit growth per Entrepreneur; +127 net US units in 2025 per FranchiseInvestorData.

value: Weekly calls with Regional Operations Manager

brands.name: CertaPro Painters
brands.notes: North America's largest residential/commercial painting franchise (parent FirstService Brands), founded and franchising since 1992 with ~335 units. Established brand with broad market availability, but the unit count declined ~9% in the most recent Entrepreneur data and there is a history of franchisee and regulatory friction.

riskFlags.title: Unit count contraction (-9%)
riskFlags.detail: Entrepreneur's 2026 directory shows 335 units with a -9% growth rate; VettedBiz separately lists only 307 US locations. Prospects should examine Item 20 outlet tables for terminations and non-renewals.

riskFlags.title: EPA lead-paint (RRP Rule) enforcement against franchisees
riskFlags.detail: Multiple CertaPro franchise locations have been cited for failing to comply with the EPA's Lead Renovation, Repair, and Painting Rule; CertaPro Painters of Maine (IDK Ventures) was fined for not providing required documents and hiring uncertified subcontractors. A 2023 suit by Marathon Resource Management also alleged breach of contract.
riskFlags.source: AI Biz Journal — CertaPro Painters lawsuit overview

riskFlags.title: Long-running franchisee gripe site alleging high failure rate and churning
riskFlags.detail: An ex-franchisee gripe campaign (certaprosucks.org, covered by Unhappy Franchisee) alleges a very high franchise failure rate and repeated reselling of failed territories. These are unverified allegations by disgruntled former franchisees, not adjudicated findings, but they have persisted for years.
riskFlags.source: Unhappy Franchisee — CertaPro Sucks gripe site coverage

nationwide.source: CertaPro Painters franchise site — Available Markets
nationwide.note: Franchisor states 'We have availability in all major and secondary markets' and lists open markets across most US states; at time of check the page showed no available markets in KY, RI, TN, UT, WY, and DC.

totalInvestmentMin.note: Franchisor's own investment page: $171,000-$302,500; Entrepreneur lists a higher max of $320,500.

totalInvestmentMax.note: Conflict: Entrepreneur 2026 lists $320,500 max; franchisor site used as primary.

royaltyPercent.note: Base royalty 6%, tiered 6/5/4% — 4% applies only at top sales tier. Prior batch value 4 superseded by verification audit 2026-06-11.

avgUnitRevenue.note: FDD Item 19-derived average yearly gross sales per VettedBiz. Conflict: other analyses of the 2025 FDD (2024 data) report $879,663 average across all 314 reporting US franchises open 12+ months, $1,159,850 for franchises open 5+ years, and ~$2.06M for the top quartile of mature units — the figure varies sharply by cohort.

totalUnits.note: VettedBiz lists 307 US locations; franchisor markets '350+ locally owned franchises in the US and Canada'.

trainingWeeks.note: Approximately 120 hours (80 classroom + 40 on-the-job) per Entrepreneur.

item19Available.note: 2025 FDD includes Item 19 gross sales representations.

veteranDiscount.note: 20% off franchise fee for veterans; VetFran participant per franchisor site.

exclusiveTerritories.note: Residential territory is exclusive (zip-code based); commercial painting is not limited to exclusive territories per franchisor.

isGrowing.note: -9% unit growth rate per Entrepreneur 2026 data.

ownerTypes.note: Absentee ownership not permitted; owners manage painting crews/subcontractors rather than painting themselves.

brands.name: College HUNKS Hauling Junk & Moving
brands.notes: Dual-revenue junk-removal and moving franchise founded 2003 in Tampa, franchising since 2007, with ~177-190 US locations. Strong Item 19 economics ($1.45M average gross revenue per location) and nationwide territory availability, but recent unit count is roughly flat-to-declining and consumer-service complaints are common.

riskFlags.title: Slight unit contraction (-2%)
riskFlags.detail: Entrepreneur's directory shows 177 units with a -2% growth rate, versus 190 US locations per VettedBiz; major metros (Chicago, Miami, Denver, Boston, Seattle) are sold out and the franchisor is offering 40% franchise-fee discounts in mid-market and single-market territories to spur growth.

riskFlags.title: Recurring consumer complaints across locations
riskFlags.detail: Review aggregators document BBB complaints (property damage during moves, late arrivals, slow refunds) and note that the franchise model produces inconsistent pricing and service quality between locations; the Tampa BBB profile has carried a C- rating.

riskFlags.title: Franchisor litigation against former franchisee (trademark)
riskFlags.detail: College Hunks sued former franchisee Victor Spaulding / Spaulding Hauling & Moving LLC in the M.D. Fla. for trademark infringement after he allegedly operated a competing mover using 'college hunks' branding post-termination; the court allowed the suit to proceed. Indicates active post-term enforcement rather than franchisee-initiated claims.

nationwide.source: College HUNKS franchise development site — Territories
nationwide.note: Franchisor lists open territories across all 50 states (US and Canada), including incentivized 'hot' markets with 40% franchise-fee discounts; several major metros are sold out.

liquidCapitalMin.note: Franchisor requires $75K liquid minimum, $200K net worth, 680 credit score; matches Entrepreneur cash requirement.

totalInvestmentMin.note: 2025 FDD Item 7 minimum $203,100. Prior batch value 158100 superseded by verification audit 2026-06-11.

franchiseFee.note: Entrepreneur lists $55,000-$75,000; the franchisor's current development site quotes $75,000 including both junk-removal and moving business lines.

royaltyPercent.note: Plus 2% brand development fee and 1% technology fee.

avgUnitRevenue.note: Average gross revenue per franchised location per the CHHJ&M 2025 FDD (Item 19), as cited on the franchisor's development site; top 25% of owners averaged $3.09M gross revenue with $309K average EBITDA. Franchise Chatter's 2024 FDD review reported $1.28M average.

totalUnits.note: VettedBiz lists 190 US locations — counts differ by source/date.

trainingWeeks.note: Approximately 101 hours total (68 on-the-job + 33 classroom) per Entrepreneur, roughly 2.5 weeks.

item19Available.note: Franchisor publicly cites 2025 FDD Item 19 figures.

multiUnitAvailable.note: 70% of franchisees own multiple territories per franchisor.

exclusiveTerritories.note: One protected territory included with each franchise.

canRunPartTime.note: Entrepreneur lists part-time operation as allowed.

isGrowing.note: -2% unit growth per Entrepreneur; franchisor is incentivizing new territories with fee discounts.

ownerTypes.note: Absentee ownership not permitted, but part-time operation is allowed per Entrepreneur.

brands.name: Home Instead
brands.notes: Leading non-medical in-home senior care franchise founded 1994 in Omaha, franchising since 1995, acquired by Honor Technology in 2021; ~1,200 units globally with 603 reporting US franchises averaging $2.6M gross revenue (2025 FDD). Low entry cost relative to AUV, but caregiver labor/wage compliance and post-acquisition franchisee relations are documented risk areas.

riskFlags.title: DOL found systemic wage violations at franchise locations
riskFlags.detail: A US Department of Labor investigation of a Bowling Green, KY Home Instead operator expanded to six additional franchise locations across Alabama, Kentucky, and Tennessee after uncovering systemic FLSA violations, recovering $201,356 in back wages for 320 caregivers. Wage-and-hour exposure is a structural risk in home care staffing.
riskFlags.source: US Department of Labor news release

riskFlags.title: Post-Honor-acquisition franchisee friction and HQ layoffs
riskFlags.detail: After Honor Technology acquired Home Instead in 2021, many franchisees objected to the platform integration and strategic direction, and Honor laid off ~15% of HQ staff in June 2023, including long-time Home Instead employees. Trade coverage in 2024 reported the relationship had substantially improved ('from cynic to optimist').

riskFlags.title: Independent franchisee association organized around Honor agreements
riskFlags.detail: An Independent Association of Home Instead Franchisees circulated a legal memorandum to franchisees regarding the Honor agreements following the 2021 acquisition — evidence of organized franchisee concern about contract changes under the new owner.
riskFlags.source: Independent Association of Home Instead Franchisees memorandum

riskFlags.title: EEOC suit against a California franchisee
riskFlags.detail: A 2023 EEOC lawsuit against R. MacArthur Corp., a Home Instead franchisee in Alameda, CA, alleged sexual and racial harassment of and retaliation against a caregiver. Franchisee-level rather than franchisor-level litigation.

nationwide.note: Listing states Home Instead 'is currently accepting inquiries from the following states' with essentially all 50 US states named; the franchisor's site (franchises.homeinstead.com) markets both new territories and existing franchises for sale in the US and Canada.

liquidCapitalMin.note: Major source conflict: Entrepreneur lists a $300,000 cash requirement (with $500,000 net worth), while Franchise Gator lists $59,000 liquid and VettedBiz $25,000 minimum cash. Entrepreneur used as primary; verify against current FDD before relying on this.

totalInvestmentMin.note: 2025 FDD Item 7 range $91,040-$269,750; matches Entrepreneur. Franchisor markets a typical new-market startup of $98K-$125K.

royaltyPercent.note: Plus $500/month technology fee and $209-$3,418/month required systems fees per 2025 FDD.

avgUnitRevenue.note: Average gross revenue across 603 US franchised businesses in operation for the full 2024 calendar year, per 2025 FDD Item 19; median was $2,261,503.

totalUnits.note: Entrepreneur worldwide count. US system is ~625 locations (619 franchised + 6 corporate per FranDB 2025 FDD data).

closureCount.note: 10 US outlets ceased operations in the most recent FDD reporting year, alongside 54 transfers and 4 franchisor reacquisitions, per FranDB's 2025 FDD Item 20 summary.

trainingWeeks.note: Approximately 45 hours total (16 on-the-job + 28.75 classroom) per Entrepreneur, i.e. about one week.

employeesRequired.note: 9-12 employees required per Entrepreneur (office staff; caregiver workforce is additional).

veteranDiscount.note: Entrepreneur lists 20% off franchise fee; Franchise Gator and 1851 report a full franchise-fee waiver for qualified veterans (limited availability, e.g. two veterans per year).

canRunFromHome.note: Entrepreneur lists home/mobile operation as possible; most owners operate from a small office staffed 9-12.

isGrowing.note: +2.6% per Entrepreneur; FranDB shows ~1% US unit growth (616 to 620) — slow but positive.

ownerTypes.note: Absentee ownership not permitted; owners manage an office team of 9-12 plus caregiver staff.
